coredata.dc:title: Enhanced energy storage in electric double-layer capacitors using boron-doped graphene and upcycled carbon quantum dots derived from spent coffee grounds as electrode materials
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Arora, Grishika
Sabran, Nuur Syahidah
Zhang, Bo
Jun, H.K.
coredata.dc:description: In the pursuit of high-efficiency and sustainable energy storage solutions, we investigate a novel electrode material: boron-doped graphene (BG) combined with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from upcycled, medium-roasted local Liberica spent coffee grounds. Boron doping of graphene is effective in imparting p-type characteristics that significantly enhance electrical conductivity and create abundant active sites for ion adsorption. This modification establishes graphene as an ideal complement to CQDs, whose integration further increases surface area and improves electron mobility. The resulting BG-CQDs composite exhibited strong synergistic properties, yielding good electrochemical properties in electric double-layer capacitors (EDLCs), as demonstrated by cyclic voltammetry and galvanostatic charge-discharge tests. At a scan rate of 5 mV/s, the BG-CQD EDLC device achieved a specific capacitance of 43.5 F/g, higher than the 3.61 F/g observed in EDLC with electrode material consists of boron-doped graphene alone. Under constant current density, the BG-CQD EDLC attained a specific capacitance of 150 F/g, an energy density of 5.2 Wh/kg, and a power density of 156.8 W/kg, showcasing the impact of boron doping on charge storage capabilities through enhanced conductivity and ion adsorption. This study underscores the pivotal role of boron-doped graphene in enhancing CQDs performance, presenting a promising composite for next-generation supercapacitors in sustainable energy storage applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Boron-doped graphene
Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Spent coffee grounds
Electric double-layer capacitors (EDLCs)

Nuur Syahidah Sabran thanks Universiti Tunku Abdul Rahman for the provision of the Post-doctoral Research Scholarship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Process flow of synthesis of CQDs.
(a) Absorption spectra and (b) Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the CQDs derived from medium-roasted local Liberica SCGs.
The XRD spectra of boron-doped graphene (BG) in activated carbon slurry and boron-doped graphene with medium-roasted local Liberica SCGs CQDs (BG-CQDs) in activated carbon slurry.
FTIR spectra of boron-doped graphene (BG) in activated carbon slurry and boron-doped graphene with medium-roasted local Liberica SCGs CQDs (BG-CQDs) in activated carbon slurry.

SEM images of (a) boron-doped graphene (BG) and (b) boron-doped graphene with medium-roasted local Liberica SCGs CQDs (BG-CQDs) in activated carbon slurry; with (c) TEM and (d) SAED of medium-roasted local Liberica SCGs CQDs.
CV of boron-doped graphene (BG) and boron-doped graphene with medium-roasted local Liberica SCGs CQDs (BG-CQDs) in activated carbon slurry measured at a scan rate of 5 mV/s.

(a) GCD of boron-doped graphene (BG) and boron-doped graphene with medium-roasted local Liberica SCGs CQDs (BG-CQDs) in activated carbon slurry (b) EIS of boron-doped graphene with medium-roasted local Liberica SCGs CQDs (BG-CQDs).

head.ce:title: Enhanced energy storage in electric double-layer capacitors using boron-doped graphene and upcycled carbon quantum dots derived from spent coffee grounds as electrode materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dSchool of Materials Science and Engineering, Tianjin Chengjian University, Tianjin 300384, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the pursuit of high-efficiency and sustainable energy storage solutions, we investigate a novel electrode material: boron-doped graphene (BG) combined with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from upcycled, medium-roasted local Liberica spent coffee grounds. Boron doping of graphene is effective in imparting p-type characteristics that significantly enhance electrical conductivity and create abundant active sites for ion adsorption. This modification establishes graphene as an ideal complement to CQDs, whose integration further increases surface area and improves electron mobility. The resulting BG-CQDs composite exhibited strong synergistic properties, yielding good electrochemical properties in electric double-layer capacitors (EDLCs), as demonstrated by cyclic voltammetry and galvanostatic charge-discharge tests. At a scan rate of 5 mV/s, the BG-CQD EDLC device achieved a specific capacitance of 43.5 F/g, higher than the 3.61 F/g observed in EDLC with electrode material consists of boron-doped graphene alone. Under constant current density, the BG-CQD EDLC attained a specific capacitance of 150 F/g, an energy density of 5.2 Wh/kg, and a power density of 156.8 W/kg, showcasing the impact of boron doping on charge storage capabilities through enhanced conductivity and ion adsorption. This study underscores the pivotal role of boron-doped graphene in enhancing CQDs performance, presenting a promising composite for next-generation supercapacitors in sustainable energy storage applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recent research has primarily focused on innovative materials for energy storage. Supercapacitors, known for their high capacities and rapid, reversible redox reactions, have garnered substantial interest in the energy storage sector [1–2]. These devices are classified into three types: hybrid capacitors, electric double-layer capacitors (EDLCs), and pseudocapacitors [3]. Although EDLCs have lower energy densities than mid-range batteries and face relative manufacturing cost challenges, they offer significant advantages such as long cycle life, moderate energy and power densities, rapid charge and discharge rates, high retention, good stability with no chemical or structural changes during charge storage, functionality in extreme temperatures, and superior safety. In pseudocapacitors, charge is stored electrochemically through surface Faradaic redox reactions, where electrolyte ions penetrate pores and undergo rapid, reversible surface reactions to enable charge transfer. While pseudocapacitors achieve high theoretical capacitance, practical application is limited. Increasing specific energy density often compromises both power density and cell lifespan during redox processes [4]. Hybrid supercapacitors aim to integrate the features of EDLCs and pseudocapacitors, achieving a balance of high energy density with sustained power density, extended cycle life, and cost-effectiveness. Typically, hybrid supercapacitors employ one of the three configurations: one electrode made from pseudocapacitor material and the other from EDLC material; a composite of EDLC and pseudocapacitor materials for one or both electrodes; or a device where one electrode consists of a battery-like material paired with a composite of EDLC and pseudocapacitor materials [4–6].

Batteries and EDLCs exemplify conventional energy storage technologies. EDLCs rely on electrostatic interactions within electrodes with high specific surface areas, enabling high power density. However, their energy density is generally lower than that of metal-ion batteries. Due to their electrostatic storage mechanism, EDLCs offer rapid charging, extended cycle life, and excellent retention capacity, making the enhancement of energy density and other key performance features a major research focus [7–10]. Numerous materials, including metal oxides [11], conductive polymers [12], graphene [13], carbon nanotubes [14], porous carbon from MOFs [15], doped porous carbon [16], and metal hydroxides [17], have been explored to improve supercapacitor performance. Among these, carbon-based materials have received particular attention due to their high conductivity, affordability, excellent physiochemical stability, and natural abundance [18]. These materials typically exhibit EDLC-type storage behaviour, with efficient ion adsorption and desorption kinetics primarily influenced by their porous structure and specific surface area.

The discovery of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with tunable properties has expanded the applications of carbon-based materials. CQDs, which are quasi-zero-dimensional nanomaterials, offer numerous active surface sites, excellent chemical stability, high water solubility, and versatile functionalization options [19]. Additionally, zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with heteroatom-enriched functional groups (such as oxygen, nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus, and boron) provide a high density of active sites, significantly enhancing electrochemical performance [20]. Various methods for CQD synthesis include arc discharge, laser ablation, microwave-assisted synthesis, and pyrolysis. Among these, the hydrothermal method stands out for its efficiency and eco-friendliness, making it a preferred approach. This bottom-up technique, which involves carbonizing organic precursors in a Teflon-lined hydrothermal reactor at temperatures between 180 and 200 °C, reliably produces uniformly sized particles with a high quantum yield [21]. For example, Komalavalli et al. synthesized hydroxyl‑functionalized, luminescent carbon dots (CDs) from Hibiscus sabdariffa leaves through an eight-hour hydrothermal treatment at 160 °C [22].

In the present study, CQDs were synthesized from medium-roasted local Liberica spent coffee grounds, a widely available by-product of coffee consumption. Repurposing these grounds helps reduce waste in the coffee industry, supporting a circular economy. Furthermore, biomass wastes like spent coffee grounds (SCGs) are rich in carbon and contain diverse functional groups, making them an ideal precursor for carbon-based nanomaterials [23]. This study also investigated the effects of graphene doped with heteroatom boron. Doping graphene with heteroatoms effectively modifies its electronic and chemical properties, enhancing its electrochemical performance for energy storage and conversion applications [24,25]. Boron doping introduces p-type characteristics to the hexagonal sp²-bonded carbon framework, increasing charge carrier holes and enhancing conductivity. This substitution also adjusts the bandgap, further optimizing electrical properties and promoting exceptional charge transport [26–28].

A comparative evaluation of boron-doped graphene alone and boron-doped graphene combined with medium-roasted local Liberica SCGs CQDs was conducted using cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). These techniques provide insights into the charge storage capacity, rate capability, and resistance characteristics of the materials, revealing how the synergistic effects of boron doping and CQD incorporation improve specific capacitance, reduce charge transfer resistance, and enhance cycling stability. The findings contribute to the development of sustainable, high-performance electrode materials for next-generation supercapacitors and other energy storage devices.

This study used single-origin medium-roasted Liberica SCGs, collected as biomass waste from consumer, and cleaned with ethanol without further treatment. Key materials included boron-doped graphene, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), 1-Butyl-3-methylimidazolium bis(trifluoromethylsulfonyl)imide (BMIM TFSI) ionic liquid, activated carbon, Super P conductive carbon black, polyvinylidene fluoride (PVdF), N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), aluminum substrate, and phosphoric acid (H3PO4). Except for boron-doped graphene, all materials matched those used in our previous study [23] and were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich without additional modification.

CQDs were synthesized from SCGs using an eco-friendly hydrothermal method as previously reported [23]. Briefly, 2 g of SCGs were mixed with 20 mL of distilled water, stirred at 70 °C for three hours, and then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave. The mixture was heated to 200 °C for four hours, breaking down the biomass into carbon-rich CQDs with enhanced surface functionality. After cooling, the solution was filtered, purified via 48-hour dialysis in a 12,000 Da tubing, and freeze-dried to obtain the final CQD product. This method produced CQDs with excellent dispersibility and electrochemical properties suitable for energy storage. The process flow of the synthesis of CQDs is summarized in Fig. 1 .

The preparation began by dissolving 10 wt% of PVdF in 5 mL of NMP solvent at 60 °C. Subsequently, 80 wt% of activated carbon (AC), 5 wt% of Super P, 2.5 wt% of boron-doped graphene, and 2.5 wt% of CQDs were added to the solution. The mixture was thoroughly stirred and maintained at 60 °C for 24 h Once prepared, the produced carbon slurry was uniformly applied to the aluminium current collector using a brushing technique. The coated electrodes were then dried in an oven at 120 °C for 4 h After drying, the mass of the electrodes was measured.

EDLC samples were prepared by sandwiching the gel polymer electrolyte (GPE) film between the two identical carbon electrodes that consist of boron-doped graphene and CQDs, prepared with Super P slurry. Characterization of the GPE has been reported in our previous work [28]. The cell was then placed within a sample holder for further analysis. The structure of the fabricated EDLC is illustrated in Fig. 2 .

Characterization of CQDs included a measurement with UV–Vis spectroscopy (model 100 Conc Cary) to capture absorption spectra across 200–800 nm, and photoluminescence (PL) measurement using an FS5 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh Instruments) to analyze the optical properties and quantum confinement effects. Structural analysis was performed with a Shimadzu LabX XRD-6000 X-ray diffractometer (Cu-Kα radiation, λ = 1.54056 Å) across a 2θ range of 10°–80° with a 10° step. FTIR spectra were obtained using a Nicolet iS10 FTIR Spectrum equipped with ATR (range 4000–400 cm⁻¹ at 4 cm⁻¹ resolution). Surface topography was examined via a Hitachi S-3400 N SEM, and high-resolution TEM images were captured using a Tecnai G2 F20 microscope at 200 kV. Electrochemical characterization of the EDLC included cyclic voltammetry (CV) at varied scan rates, galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) using a ZIVE SPI potentiostat, with the EDLC device secured in a sample holder. The synthesis process was also repeated to verify CQDs reproducibility.

Fig. 3 shows the absorbance and fluorescence characteristics of the medium-roasted local Liberica SCGs CQDs. While the optical properties of the CQDs may not directly influence the performance of EDLCs, they offer significant advantages for various optical applications. In Fig. 3(a), the absorption bands observed between 300 and 350 nm are attributed to π–π* transitions associated with C = C double bonds and electronic transitions in oxygen-containing groups within the CQDs [29]. Peaks in the UV region confirm the formation of CQDs, with a spectral tail extending into the visible range. The highest absorbance, recorded at 2.2 for medium-roasted Liberica SCGs at 345 nm, signifies strong light absorption capabilities and potential for use in applications requiring colored materials [30].

In Fig. 3(b), the fluorescence properties of the CQDs were evaluated by varying the excitation wavelength from 350 to 700 nm. This variation resulted in a redshift, with the emission peak intensity at 410 nm for medium-roasted Liberica SCGs. The redshift arises from π–π* transitions in the graphitic sp² domains, occurring at lower excitation energies. The emission spectrum at 470 nm begins with a notably high intensity, likely due to enhanced light absorption and emission from oxygen-containing functional groups such as carbonyl, hydroxyl, or carboxyl groups, and the presence of defects within the CQDs. The observed redshift and high fluorescence intensity highlight the material interactions, specifically between the sp² graphitic core and surface functional groups. These interactions generate multiple emission trap states, enhancing the CQDs’ photoluminescence by broadening the emission spectrum and improving their overall light absorption and emission efficiencies [31].

To evaluate the crystal structure and phase purity, boron-doped graphene (BG) and boron-doped graphene with medium-roasted local Liberica SCGs CQDs (BG-CQDs) were analyzed using XRD. The results are shown in Fig. 4 . The XRD patterns for these samples display distinct peaks within the 2θ range of 20° to 80° For BG, prominent peaks are observed at 2θ values of 26.6°, 36.4°, 43.2°, 64.7°, and 77.9°, while for BG-CQDs, peaks appear at 26.8°, 36.4°, 43.4°, 54.6°, 64.5°, and 77.6° Major peaks around 26° and 43° indicate the presence of graphitic carbon [32]. The shoulder peak near 26° corresponds to the (002) plane of carbon, while the weaker peak at 43° corresponds to the (533) plane. The addition of CQDs to BG suggests improved stacking order or alignment, likely due to the interaction between CQDs and the BG layers. This interaction indicates structural or compositional changes, suggesting that CQDs may enhance crystallinity or introduce additional phases through their interaction with the boron-doped graphene matrix [33,34].

In contrast to our previous studies [23], the XRD patterns for medium-roasted local Liberica SCGs CQDs also show well-defined peaks in a similar 2θ range, with notable peaks at 36.4°, 39.2°, 43.6°, 64.7°, and 77.9° The BG-CQDs composite demonstrates an overall structure that is largely crystalline, with sharper peaks indicating well-ordered regions. However, the presence of CQDs may introduce localized disorder or amorphous characteristics within the composite, contributing to a complex structure that combines both ordered and disordered elements in the BG matrix.

The functional groups and surface structure of BG and BG-CQDs samples were analyzed with FTIR. The FTIR results are shown in Fig. 5 . In the spectra, a band at 882.76 cm⁻¹ is attributed to the asymmetric stretching of B-O bonds, which signifies boron incorporation. Additionally, B-C bond stretching is evident at 1181.79 cm⁻¹ and 1410.74 cm⁻¹, further confirming successful boron doping within the carbon network [32]. Several other characteristic peaks were observed: the bands at 1986.85 cm⁻¹, 2161.90 cm⁻¹, and 2325.28 cm⁻¹ correspond to C=C bonds, C=O stretching, and nitrile (CN) vibrations, respectively, indicating the presence of various functional groups that can contribute to the electrochemical activity of the material [32,33,36].

In comparison, Thirumal's synthesis of boron-doped graphene nanosheets (HB-GNS) produced an FTIR spectrum with a band at 1035 cm⁻¹, also attributed to asymmetric B-O stretching. Similarly, B-C stretching was noted at 1115 cm⁻¹, affirming boron integration within the carbon structure. Additional peaks in Thirumal's spectrum at 1567 cm⁻¹, 1695 cm⁻¹, and 3403 cm⁻¹ corresponded to C=C bonds, C=O bonds, and OH stretching vibrations, respectively [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanning electron microscopy & transmission electron microscopy (SEM and TEM)
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 displays the SEM images of BG and BG-CQDs. In Fig. 6(a), BG exhibits a layered, flake-like morphology typical of graphene-based materials, with well-defined edges and large, irregularly shaped pieces. The surface is relatively smooth but shows some roughness and voids, which could enhance surface area and porosity, making it advantageous for energy storage applications. In Fig. 6(b), the addition of CQDs to BG results in a slightly more compact and rougher texture than pure BG. The CQDs appear to modify the surface, possibly by filling gaps or adhering to the graphene layers, which may improve the material's conductivity and surface area, thereby enhancing its electrochemical properties [35]. Compared to Thirumal's synthesis of HB-GNS [36], which shows white, cloudy nanosheets randomly stacked with sizes of several hundred nanometers and multi-oriented graphene layers, including some vertical alignment, the morphology observed in this study indicates a more organized structure. This arrangement may offer a balanced combination of porosity and conductivity, making the material suitable for high-performance energy storage applications. In short, integrating CQDs into boron-doped graphene seems to enhance surface roughness and compactness, likely increasing porosity and active surface area. This structural modification could improve conductivity and electrochemical performance, making the material promising for advanced energy storage applications.

Fig. 6(c) shows TEM images of the CQDs, where lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.2676 nm indicate graphitic carbon, with CQDs sizes range from 1.6 to 4.4 nm [36]. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of CQDs from medium-roasted local Liberica SCGs, shown in Fig. 6(d), reveals diffused rings, suggesting a polycrystalline nanostructure and the presence of multiple nanocrystalline domains [37]. Additionally, the TEM images display well-defined lattice fringes, supporting the crystalline characteristics of the CQDs.

CV test was employed to evaluate the performance of an EDLC device with electrodes made from BG and BG-CQDs. This electrochemical analysis measures the capacitive behaviour of the electrodes by plotting the current response against the cyclically sweeping applied voltage, forming a characteristic hysteresis loop that indicates energy storage capability. Specific capacitance values at various scan rates can be calculated using the equation:
(1)
C sp
=
1
m
×
s
×
Δ
v
∫
v 1
v 2
I
( v )
dv
where,
∫
v 1
v 2
I
(
v
)
d
v
represents the area under the CV curve, s is the scan rate, Δν is the potential window, and m is the mass of active material of single electrode [38]. Fig. 7 shows the CV curves of EDLCs with BG and BG-CQDs, combined with Super P-activated carbon slurry, over a potential range of 0 to 1 V. At a scan rate of 5 mV/s, the specific capacitance for boron-doped graphene alone was measured at 3.61 F/g, while the boron-doped graphene with CQDs achieved a significantly higher specific capacitance of 43.5 F/g. The enhanced performance can be attributed to the synergistic effects of boron doping and CQDs incorporation. Boron doping introduces p-type characteristics into the graphene structure, creating additional active sites that facilitate ion adsorption, thereby increasing charge storage capacity. Furthermore, boron doping improves the material's conductivity, enabling faster charge transfer. The integration of CQDs further enhances electron mobility and promotes ion diffusion by providing additional pathways and active sites. This combined effect of BG-CQDs significantly boosts the overall performance of the EDLC device, making it more efficient in energy storage applications. The enhanced specific capacitance values reflect the improved charge storage efficiency and conductivity, making this composite material a promising candidate for high-performance energy storage systems [39,40,41].

In comparison, our previous EDLC without CQDs achieved a specific capacitance of only 0.75 F/g at the same scan rate [28]. This notable difference underscores the significant enhancement in charge storage capacity achieved by incorporating CQDs into boron-doped graphene electrodes. While the values reported in this study may be lower than some previously documented results, it is essential to emphasize that this work primarily aimed to establish an initial proof-of-concept, showcasing effective charge storage at the electrode-electrolyte interface. The findings highlight the potential of CQDs with boron-doped graphene integration to improve the electrochemical performance of EDLC devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para:
GCD measurements were performed to evaluate the charging and discharging characteristics of the EDLC device, using a constant current density. The specific capacitance (C sp) of the device, which uses electrodes made from BG and BG-CQDs, was calculated using the formula:
(2)
C sp
=
i .
Δ
t
m .
Δ
V
where, i represents the applied current,
Δ
t
is the discharging time, m denotes the active mass of single electrode, and
Δ
V
is the voltage applied across the cell. Fig. 8 (a) illustrates that the EDLC device with electrodes composed of BG combined with Super P carbon achieved a specific capacitance (C sp) of 117.64 F/g, an energy density of 3.76 Wh/kg, and a power density of 113.2 W/kg at a current density of 0.5 A/g. By incorporating CQDs with the BG electrodes, the BG-CQDs device demonstrated a significantly enhanced discharge C sp of 150 F/g, with an increased energy density of 5.2 Wh/kg and a power density of 156.8 W/kg. This improvement indicates that the addition of CQDs effectively enhances the device's charge storage capabilities, energy density, and power output, likely due to the increased surface area and enhanced electron mobility provided by the CQDs.

In contrast, a related study by Pallavolu et al. [42] investigated a symmetric supercapacitor using sulfur-doped CQDs and achieved a maximum C sp of 55.7 F/g at a higher current density of 1 A/g, along with an energy density of 15.2 Wh/kg and a power density of 700 W/kg. Additionally, in our previous study without CQDs integration, the EDLC achieved the C sp of 45.8 F/g, an energy density of 1.85 Wh/kg, and a power density of 55.8 W/kg [28]. This past result demonstrates effective charge storage and decent performance, though it is notably lower than the values observed in the BG-CQDs configuration in this study. These comparisons highlight the efficiency of using boron-doped graphene with CQDs derived from spent coffee grounds, underscoring the potential of CQDs as a valuable additive for enhancing energy storage and discharge performance in graphene-based supercapacitors. Moreover, the use of CQDs derived from waste materials provides a sustainable approach by upcycling organic waste for energy storage applications.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted to assess the internal resistance characteristics of the BG-CQDs electrode configuration. The Randles circuit model, commonly used to interpret EIS results (Fig. 8(b)), was applied for the analysis. In this equivalent circuit, a parallel combination of charge transfer resistance (R b) and double-layer capacitance (CPE1) represents the semicircular portion of the impedance response, while the series resistor (R s) and a constant phase element (CPE2) account for the depressed semicircle and the linear (45-degree) Warburg impedance behaviour [23]. The optimized BG-CQDs configuration yielded a charge transfer resistance (R a) of 3.2 Ω and a bulk resistance (R ab) of 5.1 Ω. Comparatively, the previous EDLC device without CQDs exhibited much higher resistances, with R a and R ab values of 65.2 Ω and 36.5 kΩ, respectively [28], indicating a substantial improvement in conductivity and ion transport upon the incorporation of CQDs.

BMIM TFSI in polymer electrolyte significantly enhances the performance of BG-CQD EDLCs by acting as a plasticizer and offering high ionic conductivity. Its plasticizing effect improves electrode wetting and ion mobility, enabling efficient charge storage, while its high conductivity facilitates rapid ion transport, reduces internal resistance, and supports faster charge/discharge rates. In comparison, aqueous electrolytes (e.g., KOH or H₂SO₄) provide high ionic conductivity and low resistance for faster charge/discharge rates but are restricted by a narrow voltage window (∼1.2 V), limiting energy density. Organic electrolytes offer a broader voltage range (∼2.7–3.0 V), improving energy density, though lower ionic conductivity and potential material compatibility issues can affect performance. Additionally, BMIM TFSI's wide electrochemical stability window, measured at 3.09 V in our previous study [26], allows for higher energy density and operational voltage, ensuring enhanced energy efficiency and prolonged cycle life making it an excellent choice for high-performance and durable devices. The electrolyte concentration and temperature also play critical roles, with higher concentrations improving ionic conductivity but potentially increasing viscosity, and elevated temperatures enhancing ion mobility while possibly accelerating material degradation. Optimizing these factors ensures the best balance between efficiency, stability, and durability. For reference, Pallavolu's [42] sulfur-doped CQDs-based symmetric supercapacitor had the R a of 8.2 Ω, and Thirumal's [36] synthesized HB-GS electrode achieved the R a of 3.52 Ω. These results confirm the superior performance of the BG-CQDs configuration in terms of minimized internal resistance, enhanced conductivity, and effective charge transfer, further validating the advantages of boron-doped graphene with CQDs incorporation for EDLC.

While the current data demonstrates promising results, this study focuses primarily on the initial electrochemical performance of the BG-CQD composite, including its specific capacitance, energy density, and power density. Long-term cycling stability, however, was not investigated in this work. Based on the robust structural properties of boron-doped graphene and the enhanced dispersibility and functionality of CQDs, we anticipate that the BG-CQD composite will exhibit comparable or superior cycling stability to conventional carbon-based EDLCs. The results of a separate long-term stability study will be shared in a future publication.

The CQDs themselves play a pivotal role in redefining EDLC performance. Their small size and surface functional groups synergize effectively with boron-doped graphene, enhancing conductivity, charge transport, and active surface area for ion adsorption. Optimizing the CQD-to-graphene ratio could further improve performance by balancing conductivity, surface area, and ion accessibility. Moreover, the size and surface chemistry of CQDs—particularly those with functional groups like hydroxyl and carboxyl—significantly influence their interaction with graphene and electrolytes. These properties improve dispersion, electrode wettability, and ion mobility, paving the way for even greater advancements in energy storage technology.

Despite the promising initial performance of the BG-CQD EDLC, potential degradation mechanisms over extended cycling must be considered. In conventional EDLCs, degradation typically arises from structural fatigue, electrolyte interactions, and material detachment. For the BG-CQD composite, structural fatigue could occur as the high surface area and porous framework undergo repeated ion insertion and extraction, potentially reducing active sites for ion adsorption [43–44]. Additionally, chemical interactions between the electrode surface and the electrolyte may lead to the formation of passivation layers or the loss of active sites. Another concern is the potential detachment of CQDs from the boron-doped graphene matrix, which could compromise charge storage efficiency. Nevertheless, the unique properties of boron-doped graphene, such as enhanced structural robustness, high conductivity, and strong interfacial interactions with CQDs, are expected to mitigate these risks and improve durability compared to conventional materials.

In terms of manufacturing, the synthesis of CQDs can be optimized by employing faster and more energy-efficient methods, such as microwave-assisted or low-temperature hydrothermal processes. Scaling up production through bulk synthesis techniques and utilizing abundant waste precursors, like spent coffee grounds or other biomass, could significantly reduce material costs. Simplifying post-processing steps, such as replacing energy-intensive dialysis and freeze-drying with efficient filtration techniques, would also enhance cost-efficiency [45–46]. Integrating BG-CQD composite synthesis directly into electrode fabrication could eliminate intermediate steps, further reducing time and expenses. The eco-friendly hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs requires minimal chemical inputs and energy, making it a cost-effective process. Additionally, recycling solvents and reagents could contribute to both sustainability and cost reduction. These strategies will be explored in future studies to ensure cost optimization without compromising the excellent electrochemical performance of the BG-CQD composite.

The environmental impact of this innovation lies in its sustainable and eco-conscious production practices [47]. By utilizing biomass feedstocks like SCGs, the synthesis process minimizes ecological harm while converting waste into valuable resources. However, challenges such as energy-intensive processes and the use of hazardous reagents must be carefully managed to avoid adverse environmental effects. Proper disposal strategies will also be critical, ensuring compliance with environmental standards and addressing potential toxicity or harmful degradation products. For instance, the environmental footprint of producing CQDs and boron-doped graphene can be further reduced by optimizing energy-efficient methods, such as hydrothermal synthesis, and adhering to green chemistry principles. Incorporating closed-loop systems for solvent recycling, effective waste management strategies, and scalable automated processes will contribute to a more sustainable production model. Conducting life cycle analyses (LCA) will help identify critical areas for improvement, ensuring a balance between environmental stewardship and high-performance standards in energy storage applications. Robust waste management practices will also mitigate the risks associated with nanomaterial release, safeguarding human health and ecosystems.

This study presents a transformative approach to enhancing EDLC performance by integrating boron-doped graphene (BG) with CQDs synthesized from upcycled medium-roasted local Liberica spent coffee grounds (SCGs). Through an eco-friendly and straightforward hydrothermal process, waste coffee grounds were converted into CQDs, providing a sustainable pathway to high-performance materials. Boron doping proved to be a effective in introducing additional active sites, and significantly enhancing electrical conductivity. These features facilitated efficient ion adsorption, superior charge capacity, and created a robust framework for CQD incorporation. Notably, the BG-CQDs composite achieved low charge transfer resistance (Ra) of 3.2 Ω, a significant improvement over the 65.2 Ω observed in BG-based EDLCs without CQDs.

The BG-CQDs composite EDLC also demonstrated outstanding electrochemical performance, including a specific capacitance of 150 F/g, an energy density of 5.2 Wh/kg, and a power density of 156.8 W/kg at a current density of 0.5 A/g. These results underscore its excellent charge storage capacity and rapid energy release—crucial attributes for next-generation supercapacitors. The synergy between the composite's minimal internal resistance, high surface area, and enhanced conductivity enabled rapid electron transport and efficient charge storage. This improved material composition sets a new benchmark for energy storage technology, offering a sustainable and efficient alternative to conventional supercapacitor materials while contributing to environmental waste reduction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Research data policy and data availability statements
ce:section.ce:para: Data generated and/or analyzed during the study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

Grishika Arora: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Nuur Syahidah Sabran: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Bo Zhang: Writing – review & editing. H.K. Jun: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research was supported by the Ministry of Higher Education (MoHE) Malaysia, through the Fundamental Research Grant Scheme (FRGS/1/2022/TK08/UTAR/02/7). Nuur Syahidah Sabran thanks Universiti Tunku Abdul Rahman for the provision of the Post-doctoral Research Scholarship.
